does: This paper presents an empirical re-evaluation of WorkBench, a benchmark for assessing workplace agent performance, comparing results from March 2024 against June 2026. The authors test Claude Opus 4.8, the frontier model at the time of their 2026 evaluation, finding it completes 89% of tasks with only 2.5% unintended harmful actions, substantially improving upon GPT-4's 43% completion rate and 26% harmful action rate from two years prior. The paper documents the distribution and types of errors across different model classes, analyzes the relationship between task completion and safety outcomes, and provides cost-performance comparisons across proprietary and open-weight models, releasing an updated benchmark version with improved data quality and expanded model evaluations.
connects: This work extends the original WorkBench benchmark, which pioneered systematic evaluation of autonomous agents in realistic workplace scenarios rather than isolated tasks. It builds on the growing literature evaluating frontier language models on agent benchmarks and their propensity for harmful actions, connecting to work on agent safety, alignment, and unintended consequences of deployed systems. The paper situates itself within the trajectory of advances in large language models and their increased reasoning capabilities, responding to earlier concerns about capability-safety tradeoffs in agent systems. It also engages with the emerging literature comparing proprietary and open-weight model performance economics, extending prior analyses of model scaling and cost efficiency.
enables: This updated benchmark enables future work to establish clearer baselines for agent safety and capability progression, facilitating longitudinal studies of model improvement trajectories. The documented persistence of certain error classes—particularly email misdirection—points toward specific areas where current approaches remain brittle and guides targeted safety interventions. The finding that capability and safety improvements align rather than conflict suggests promising directions for unified training approaches rather than adversarial optimization. The cost-performance data on open-weight models enables researchers to make more informed choices about model deployment and creates benchmarks for assessing when open-weight alternatives become viable replacements for proprietary systems, potentially accelerating the evaluation pace for future agent benchmarking efforts.
